title: Database Fundamentals
description: SQL indexing, transaction isolation levels, normal forms, NoSQL taxonomy, query optimization, and storage engines — everything needed for FAANG database interview questions.
details:
FAANG interview strategy: Database questions span the full stack — from disk-level index structures to distributed consistency models. The most common pitfalls: (1) recommending an index without checking write-to-read ratio, (2) confusing isolation levels with their anomaly guarantees, (3) picking NoSQL without considering transaction requirements, (4) ignoring the query planner when optimizing. Always start any database design answer by clarifying the workload: OLTP (row-oriented, fast writes, point queries) vs OLAP (column-oriented, large scans, aggregations). Most FAANG databases questions are OLTP with a specific read/write ratio they'll give you. Memorize the B-tree fanout formula, the isolation anomaly matrix, and the DynamoDB paper's consistency model — these are the three most-asked topics.

Edge case to watch for: When asked about indexes, interviewers often hide the write pattern. An index on a monotonically increasing column (auto-increment ID, timestamp) causes B-tree page splits at the rightmost leaf — every insert touches the same page, creating a hot spot and contention. Hash-based indexes or a different key order avoids this. Always ask about write distribution before recommending an index strategy.

sections.title: SQL Indexing
sections.description:
Indexes are data structures that speed up data retrieval at the cost of write overhead and storage. Choosing the right index type is the #1 query optimization technique.

B-Tree (Balanced Tree): The default index in every major RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, Oracle). Stores key-value pairs sorted by key, with leaf nodes containing pointers to heap tuples (or the actual data in clustered indexes). Fanout formula: Each node stores O(order) keys, where order = page_size / key_size. A typical 8KB page with 16-byte keys + 8-byte pointers gives order ≈ 8KB / 24 ≈ 341 keys per node. Search depth: depth = logfanout(N) = log341(109) ≈ 3.6, so ~4 seeks for a billion-row table — hence “B-trees read any row in 3-4 I/Os.”

Hash Index: Maps keys directly to heap locations via a hash function. O(1) lookup for point queries (WHERE id = 42). Cannot support range queries (WHERE id > 42), ordering (ORDER BY), or prefix matching. Used in PostgreSQL (USING HASH), MySQL MEMORY engine. Good for key-value lookups; bad for anything else.

GiST (Generalized Search Tree): A balanced tree that supports custom data types and operators. Used for full-text search (TSVECTOR), geometric data (PostGIS with R-Tree behavior), and range types (int4range). More flexible than B-tree but slower for simple equality queries.

GIN (Generalized Inverted Index): An “inverted index” — stores (key, posting list) pairs. Optimized for composite values: array contains (@>), JSONB path queries (jsonb_path_ops), full-text lexeme search. GIN builds a B-tree over keys, each pointing to a list of row IDs. Slow to build/insert (pending list + fast update can mitigate), but extremely fast for “find rows where column CONTAINS value.”

Clustered vs Non-Clustered: A clustered index determines the physical order of rows on disk. The table data is stored at the leaf level of the B-tree. Each table can have exactly one clustered index (InnoDB's PRIMARY KEY). A non-clustered index stores the indexed columns + a pointer (row ID or clustering key) to find the actual row. Non-clustered lookups require two B-tree traversals: the secondary index first, then the clustered index (a “key lookup” or “bookmark lookup”).

Composite Indexes (multi-column): Index on (A, B, C). The leftmost prefix rule: the index can only be used for queries that filter on a leftmost prefix of the indexed columns. WHERE A = 1 &x2713; — uses first column. WHERE A = 1 AND B = 2 &x2713;. WHERE B = 2 &x2717; — cannot use the index because A is skipped. For range conditions, order matters: WHERE A = 1 AND B > 5 AND C = 3 uses A and B for the index scan but not C (B's range breaks the prefix). Solution: reorder columns so equality predicates come before range predicates.

When indexes hurt: (1) Write amplification: Every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on an indexed table must update every index. With 5 indexes, a single row insert writes 6 B-trees (1 heap + 5 indexes). (2) Low cardinality columns: Indexing a boolean column yields ~50% selectivity — a sequential scan is cheaper than the index traversal overhead. PostgreSQL and Oracle have bitmap scans for this case (multiple low-cardinality filters combined). (3) Index maintenance during VACUUM/cleanup: PostgreSQL's MVTO creates dead tuples that VACUUM must clean in every index.

Query Plan Analysis: Three scan types from cheapest to most expensive per row returned: (a) Seq Scan: Sequential full-table read. Best when >10-20% of rows are selected. (b) Index Scan: Traverse the B-tree to find matching tuples, then access the heap for columns not in the index. Each row hit costs one random I/O. (c) Index-Only Scan: All requested columns exist in the index itself (the index is a “covering index”). The heap is never touched. Drastically faster. Visible in PostgreSQL EXPLAIN as Index Only Scan with Heap Fetches: 0.

Covering Indexes: An index that includes all columns needed by a query (“covers” the query). In PostgreSQL: CREATE INDEX ON orders (customer_id) INCLUDE (total, status). MySQL: CREATE INDEX idx ON orders (customer_id, total, status) — but this also affects ordering. Covering indexes enable Index-Only Scans and are the single most impactful optimization for read-heavy workloads.

sections.title: ACID Transactions
sections.description:
ACID is the set of properties that guarantee database transactions are processed reliably. Violating ACID means losing data or violating application invariants.

A — Atomicity: A transaction is an indivisible unit: all operations succeed (COMMIT) or none take effect (ROLLBACK). If the system crashes mid-transaction, the partial writes must be undone. Implemented via undo logs: before modifying a page, the database writes the old value to the undo log. On rollback or crash recovery, the undo log is replayed to restore the original state. In PostgreSQL, this is the clog (commit log) + page-level undo via t_xmin/t_xmax MVCC fields.

C — Consistency: Every transaction brings the database from one valid state to another, preserving all declared invariants (foreign keys, unique constraints, CHECK constraints, application-level invariants). Consistency is largely the application's responsibility — the DB only enforces constraints you declare. The DB cannot enforce “balance ≥ 0” unless a CHECK constraint exists.

I — Isolation: Concurrent transactions must appear to execute serially. The DBMS hides intermediate states of uncommitted transactions from others. See Isolation Levels section for the four standard levels and their anomalies.

D — Durability: Once a transaction is committed, its changes survive crashes, power loss, and disk failures. Implemented via Write-Ahead Logging (WAL): before any database page is modified, the change is written to an append-only sequential log on disk. On crash recovery, the DB replays WAL to reconstruct committed transactions. The WAL is flushed to disk at every COMMIT (fsync). PostgreSQL's full_page_writes ensures pages partially written during a crash are recoverable.

WAL (Write-Ahead Log) — The core mechanism: Every modification generates a WAL record (LSN — Log Sequence Number). The rule: WAL record must reach stable storage before the corresponding data page is written to disk (“WAL-before-data”). This guarantees that on recovery, the DB can replay any committed transaction that was lost from the data pages. PostgreSQL WAL records are 24-byte headers + payload. Checkpointing periodically flushes all dirty pages to disk and advances the “redo point” — truncating the WAL to reclaim space.

Undo vs Redo Logs: Undo logs (rollback segments) store old values for uncommitted changes — used for ROLLBACK and MVCC (providing snapshot isolation by keeping old row versions). Redo logs (WAL) store new values for committed changes — used for crash recovery. InnoDB stores both in the system tablespace. PostgreSQL uses WAL for redo; MVCC provides undo implicitly via multiple row versions.

sections.title: Isolation Levels & Anomalies
sections.description:
The SQL standard defines four isolation levels, each permitting a subset of read phenomena. Higher isolation = fewer anomalies + less concurrency + more lock contention.

Read Uncommitted: Transactions see uncommitted changes of other transactions. Permits Dirty Reads (reading a row modified by an uncommitted transaction). Practically unused — PostgreSQL maps it to Read Committed. Dangerous for any real application.

Read Committed: Each query sees only committed data at the moment the query starts. Prevents dirty reads. Permits Non-Repeatable Reads (same row read twice in one transaction yields different values — another transaction committed an update between the reads). Default in PostgreSQL and Oracle.

Repeatable Read: Every query in the transaction sees a snapshot of the data as of the first query. Prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads. Permits Phantom Reads (a query returns different sets of rows on re-execution — another transaction inserted/deleted rows that satisfy the WHERE clause). Default in MySQL/InnoDB. PostgreSQL's Repeatable Read actually prevents phantoms via its MVCC implementation (SIREAD locks or conflict detection), exceeding the SQL standard.

Serializable: Transactions execute as if run serially (one after another). Prevents all anomalies. Implemented via (a) Strict 2PL (Two-Phase Locking): MySQL/InnoDB uses next-key locks (record + gap locks) to prevent phantoms at this level. (b) SSI (Serializable Snapshot Isolation): PostgreSQL uses predicate locks and conflict detection to abort transactions that would produce non-serializable outcomes. SSI allows more concurrency than 2PL but has higher abort rates under contention.

Snapshot Isolation (MVCC): Most modern databases implement Isolation via MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control), not locking. Each transaction sees a “snapshot” of committed data at its start time. Writes create new row versions. Readers never block writers, writers never block readers. PostgreSQL's default (Read Committed) takes a new snapshot per statement. PostgreSQL's Repeatable Read takes one snapshot per transaction. Oracle: Uses Read Committed with snapshot isolation (no dirty reads, no non-repeatable reads for single statements).

Anomaly Matrix:

Isolation LevelDirty ReadNon-Repeatable ReadPhantom ReadSerialization Anomaly
Read UncommittedPossiblePossiblePossiblePossible
Read CommittedNot possiblePossiblePossiblePossible
Repeatable ReadNot possibleNot possiblePossiblePossible
SerializableNot possibleNot possibleNot possibleNot possible

Real-world defaults: PostgreSQL — Read Committed. MySQL/InnoDB — Repeatable Read (replica-safe for statement-based binlog). Oracle — Read Committed with Snapshot Isolation (what it calls “Serializable” is actually Snapshot). SQL Server — Read Committed (with READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT option for snapshot-based RC).

sections.title: Database Normalization & Denormalization
sections.description:
Normalization eliminates data redundancy and update anomalies through a series of increasingly strict normal forms. Denormalization trades write efficiency for read speed.

1NF (First Normal Form): Every column contains atomic (indivisible) values. No repeating groups or arrays. A column cannot store a list of phone numbers — you need a separate phone_numbers table or at least separate columns (phone_1, phone_2). All rows must have the same number of columns. Modern databases relax this (PostgreSQL array types, JSONB), but the form still applies for relational integrity.

2NF (Second Normal Form): Must be in 1NF AND every non-key column must be fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key (no partial dependencies). Relevant only for composite keys. Example: Orders(product_id, order_id, product_name, quantity) — product_name depends only on product_id, not on order_id. Violates 2NF. Fix: split into Orders(order_id, product_id, quantity) and Products(product_id, product_name).

3NF (Third Normal Form): Must be in 2NF AND every non-key column must depend on nothing but the primary key (no transitive dependencies). Example: Employees(emp_id, dept_id, dept_name) — dept_name depends on dept_id, which depends on emp_id. This is a transitive dependency. Fix: Employees(emp_id, dept_id) and Departments(dept_id, dept_name). 3NF is the minimum for most production OLTP schemas.

BCNF (Boyce-Codd Normal Form): A stronger version of 3NF: every determinant (column on which another column is functionally dependent) must be a candidate key. Handles edge cases 3NF misses. Example: a table with (student, subject, professor) where each professor teaches exactly one subject, and each student takes a subject with a professor. professor → subject, but professor is not a candidate key. Violates BCNF. Fix: decompose into (student, professor) and (professor, subject).

4NF (Fourth Normal Form): Must be in BCNF AND have no multi-valued dependencies (two or more independent multi-valued facts about an entity in the same table). Example: Developers(dev_id, language, certification) — languages and certifications are independent multi-valued attributes of a developer. Every language is paired with every certification, causing massive redundancy. Fix: separate tables DevLanguages(dev_id, language) and DevCerts(dev_id, certification).

Denormalization: Deliberately adding redundancy to reduce JOINs and improve read performance. Tradeoffs: (a) Write cost: Updates must touch multiple copies (application or trigger-level). (b) Data inconsistency: Copies can drift if not kept in sync. (c) Storage: More disk. Use denormalization when: read-to-write ratio > 100:1, the query involves 4+ table joins, or you need sub-millisecond reads (caching layer). Common patterns: pre-computed aggregates, materialized views, column appendages (adding order_count to customers).

sections.title: NoSQL Taxonomy & Consistency Models
sections.description:
NoSQL databases abandon the relational model to achieve scale, flexibility, or specialized query capabilities that RDBMS cannot provide efficiently. The four main categories correspond to different data access patterns.

Key-Value Stores (Redis, DynamoDB, Memcached, etcd): Simplest NoSQL model — a giant hash table. O(1) lookups by key. Best for: session stores, caching, user profiles, shopping carts. Redis: In-memory with optional persistence (RDB snapshots, AOF log). Supports data structures (lists, sets, sorted sets, hyperloglogs). Single-threaded event loop (atomic operations but no multi-key transactions). DynamoDB: Managed, serverless, auto-scaling. Partition key + optional sort key. Supports strongly consistent reads (at extra cost) and eventually consistent reads (default). DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) for microsecond reads.

Document Stores (MongoDB, Firestore, Couchbase): Stores semi-structured data as JSON/BSON documents with schemaless or schema-on-read design. Best for: content management, catalogs, event logging, flexible-schema applications. MongoDB: Uses BSON (binary JSON) with a rich query language (aggregation pipeline, geospatial, text search). Supports secondary indexes, sharding, and replication. WiredTiger storage engine provides document-level locking and compression. Weakness: multi-document transactions exist (v4.0+) but are slow compared to RDBMS. Firestore: Serverless, real-time sync, strong consistency (unusual for NoSQL). Good for mobile/web apps needing live updates.

Columnar/Column-Family Stores (Bigtable, Cassandra, HBase, Scylla): Rows but grouped into column families, optimized for wide rows and range scans. Best for: time-series data, IoT sensor data, recommendation engines, any workload with a natural partition key and sorted range queries. Bigtable (Google): Sparse, distributed, persistent multi-dimensional sorted map — keyed by (row key, column key, timestamp). Foundation for Google Search, Maps, Gmail. Cassandra: Peer-to-peer (no master), tunable consistency (ANY, ONE, QUORUM, ALL), supports linear writes scaling. Wide rows with clustering columns for sorted access within a partition. Write-optimized: sequential writes to commit log + memtable, SSTable flush. Read path: merge memtable + bloom filter + SSTables.

Graph Stores (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB): Nodes (entities) connected by edges (relationships), both with properties. Best for: social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, supply chain. Neo4j: Native graph storage with index-free adjacency (each node stores pointers to its neighbors) — O(1) traversals per hop. Cypher query language for pattern matching. Amazon Neptune: Managed, supports both Property Graph (Gremlin) and RDF (SPARQL). Used for identity graphs, network analysis.

When to use each: Key-Value for high-throughput point lookups (cache, session). Document for flexible schemas and moderate queries (catalog, CMS). Columnar for time-series and analytics (logs, metrics). Graph for connected data (social, fraud, knowledge). If you need multi-key ACID transactions, secondary indexes, or JOINs — reconsider NoSQL and use an RDBMS instead.

Consistency Models in NoSQL: NoSQL databases generally favor availability and partition tolerance over strong consistency (per the CAP theorem). Eventual consistency: After a write, reads may return stale data for a window (convergence). DynamoDB's default. Strong (linearizable) consistency: Reads always return the latest write. DynamoDB's optional ConsistentRead (higher latency, lower throughput). Tunable consistency (Cassandra): Set CL.QUORUM for each operation — choose between latency and correctness. Read repair & hinted handoff: Techniques to reconcile stale replicas. The DynamoDB paper (2007, DeCandia et al.): Defined the design space for eventually consistent key-value stores — consistent hashing (virtual nodes), vector clocks for conflict resolution, gossip-based membership, hinted handoff, Merkle trees for anti-entropy. This paper and the Bigtable paper (2006) directly influenced every NoSQL database that followed.

sections.title: Query Optimization & Execution Plans
sections.description:
Query optimization is the process of choosing the most efficient execution plan for a given SQL statement. The query planner considers dozens of plan alternatives and picks the one with the lowest estimated cost.

EXPLAIN Plans: Every RDBMS shows the query plan via EXPLAIN (PostgreSQL) or EXPLAIN ANALYZE (also executes the query and shows actual vs estimated row counts and timings). Key plan nodes: Seq Scan (full table scan), Index Scan (B-tree traversal + heap access), Index Only Scan (all columns in index, no heap), Bitmap Index Scan (PostgreSQL: build a bitmap of matching page IDs, then fetch pages), Nested Loop, Hash Join, Merge Join, Aggregate, Sort, Materialize. Look for: (a) large row count mismatches between estimated and actual (stale statistics — run ANALYZE), (b) sequential scans on large tables (missing index), (c) Sort nodes on unindexed columns, (d) “Rows Removed by Filter” much higher than “Rows Returned” (predicate pushdown or index problems).

Join Strategies: Three fundamental join algorithms, each optimal for different data distributions:

  • Nested Loop Join: For each row in the outer table, scan the inner table for matches. O(|R| × |S|) worst case. Best when one table is small (the “driving” table) and the join column is indexed on the inner table. PostgreSQL calls this Nested Loop; with batched execution (prefetch blocks of inner rows) it becomes Nested Loop with Batched Inner.
  • Hash Join: Build a hash table on the smaller table, probe with rows from the larger table. O(|R| + |S|) — amortized. Best when tables are moderately sized and no useful index exists on the join column. The hash table must fit in memory (work_mem in PostgreSQL); exceeding it spills to disk in batches.
  • Merge Join: Both inputs sorted on the join key, then merged in a single pass (like merging two sorted arrays). O(|R| + |S|). Best when both tables are already sorted (e.g., by an index scan with ORDER BY). Requires a sort step if inputs are not pre-sorted.

Index Selection: The planner evaluates each potential index using its statistics (histograms, most-common-values, correlation between column and physical order). PostgreSQL's pg_stats view and MySQL's INFORMATION_SCHEMA expose these. Key index properties: (a) Selectivity: fraction of rows matching a predicate. High selectivity (low match count) favors index scans. Low selectivity (large fraction of rows) favors seq scans or bitmap scans. (b) Correlation: how well the index order correlates with physical storage order. A perfectly correlated B-tree index matches sequential I/O — PostgreSQL charges lower cost.

Query Rewriting — Subqueries vs JOINs vs CTEs: All three can express the same logic but the optimizer treats them differently. JOINs are most optimizer-friendly — the planner can reorder them freely. Subqueries in WHERE/EXISTS clauses can be flattened into joins (semi-joins, anti-joins) by most optimizers — check EXPLAIN to confirm. Correlated subqueries (reference outer query columns) are dangerous — they execute per row of the outer query (O(N×M)). Rewrite as JOIN + GROUP BY or use a window function. CTEs (WITH clauses): In PostgreSQL, CTEs are optimization fences — the materialized CTE result is evaluated once and treated as opaque. This prevents predicate pushdown and join reordering. PostgreSQL v12+ allows NOT MATERIALIZED to inline the CTE. MySQL CTEs are not materialized and are flattened by the optimizer. Rule: Use CTEs for readability, but check EXPLAIN for fence behavior. Prefer subqueries or JOINs for performance-critical paths.

Materialized Views: A query whose result is stored as a physical table and refreshed on demand. Best for expensive aggregations that don't need real-time freshness (daily reports, dashboard numbers). PostgreSQL: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv AS SELECT ... REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY mv (requires a unique index). Automatic materialized views (Oracle, SQL Server, ClickHouse) update incrementally on base-table changes. Tradeoffs: storage cost, refresh lag, refresh contention (exclusive lock without CONCURRENTLY). Use when: the base query runs in minutes but needs sub-second response, the aggregation reduces row count by >100×, or the query joins 5+ tables for a dashboard.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Designing Data-Intensive Applications (Kleppmann, 2017) — Chapters 1-6: storage engines, replication, partitioning, transactions, the definitive database reference
  • CockroachDB: What is Distributed SQL? — Comprehensive guide to distributed SQL databases and the NewSQL movement
  • PostgreSQL Documentation — The gold standard for open-source RDBMS documentation, MVCC, indexing, and query planning
  • AWS Builders' Library — Database patterns at Amazon scale: DynamoDB, RDS, Aurora architecture
  • Spanner: Google's Globally-Distributed Database — TrueTime, external consistency, and distributed SQL at global scale
  • System Design Interview Repository — Database design patterns and scalability strategies for FAANG interviews
